patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but you learn best with step-by-step explanations and can get overwhelmed by loud/fast questioning. Start by saying: “I’ve been having more seizures lately, and I keep feeling really tired.” Early in the visit, volunteer: more seizures than usual, wiped out even after sleep, blood sugars running high despite your plan, and on-and-off headaches this month. If asked, describe episodes of staring and lip smacking for about a minute with confusion after, a few falls, thirst/peeing a lot, slight unintentional weight loss, triggers (bright lights, lack of sleep), constipation/bloating, loud snoring and sometimes waking up gasping. Do not mention unless specifically asked: learning difficulties, sensory overwhelm, choking/coughing with thin liquids, recurrent ear/hearing issues, not noticing low blood sugar until severe, irregular/heavy periods. Resist sharing bedwetting after seizures and feeling down/anxious unless the clinician normalizes it and asks gently. Express misconceptions: worry about brain tumor, wonder if spells are panic attacks, think thirst/peeing might be a UTI. If shown empathy, you relax, admit embarrassment, and ask about costs; voice fears about genetic testing affecting insurance/jobs and assume specialists are too expensive unless reassured about assistance programs.

non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular junction transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products, and certain beta-blockers) unless essential and closely monitored
Provide an emergency plan for respiratory/bulbar exacerbations (seek urgent care for dyspnea, weak cough, inability to handle secretions, or rapidly worsening weakness)

Vaccination and infection-prevention counseling; early treatment of respiratory infections with non-NMJ-worsening antibiotics when possible

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “My face and ankles have been getting puffy, and my pee looks kind of dark and foamy.” Early in the visit, volunteer: morning puffiness around the eyes, ankle/foot swelling by day’s end, tea-colored urine, foamy urine, and increased fatigue. If asked, add: peeing less despite same fluids, 6-lb weight gain in a week, headaches and occasional pounding heartbeat, mild shortness of breath with stairs or lying flat, occasional stinging with urination, and a sore throat 2 weeks ago.

Do not mention unless directly asked: frequent ibuprofen use, higher BP readings at pharmacy, nighttime itching, waking to pee small amounts, decreased appetite/foods tasting “off.” Be resistant/reluctant about: workout supplements/fat burners, new sexual partner with inconsistent condom use, and one-time cocaine use—admit only if specifically asked in a nonjudgmental way.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe I’m just dehydrated,” “maybe salty takeout/sitting caused swelling,” “could it be a kidney stone or UTI?” Voice cost/time-off worries and suspicion about “too many tests.” If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and become more willing to share details.

medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 1 tablet PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) which can worsen kidney function and cause edema
Trace pyuria/leukocyte esterase that can mimic urinary tract infection despite negative nitrites
Oral contraceptive use with edema/weight fluctuation potentially masking fluid retention from nephritis
Mild anemia that could be attributed to menstruation or chronic inflammation rather than renal disease

acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to acute interstitial nephritis
required_evidence: Acute/subacute rise in serum creatinine with reduced eGFR consistent with AKI
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with tubular dysfunction (e.g., non–anion gap metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate)
Absence of features suggesting nephrotic syndrome (serum albumin not markedly low) or advanced chronic kidney disease pattern
Clinical context compatible with medication-triggered kidney inflammation (recent exposure to common offending drugs such as antibiotics, NSAIDs, PPIs) even if not explicitly listed

expected_lab_results.blood_urea_nitrogen_bun: Elevated; BUN:creatinine ratio often not markedly high (commonly ~10–20:1)
expected_lab_results.serum_albumin: Usually normal or mildly decreased (not nephrotic-range hypoalbuminemia)
expected_lab_results.sodium: Often normal; may be mildly low with volume issues
expected_lab_results.potassium: May be elevated if AKI is moderate/severe
expected_lab_results.chloride: May be elevated if non–anion gap metabolic acidosis present
expected_lab_results.bicarbonate_co2: Often low (metabolic acidosis; can be non–anion gap due to tubular dysfunction)

medications: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO once daily (max 60 mg/day) for 1–2 weeks, then taper over 4–6 weeks if kidney function fails to improve after stopping offending agent and AIN is strongly suspected/confirmed
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO three times daily (titrate to maintain serum bicarbonate >= 22 mmol/L) if metabolic acidosis present
Furosemide 20–40 mg PO/IV once or twice daily as needed for volume overload (avoid if hypovolemic)
non_pharmacological: Stop suspected offending medication(s) immediately (avoid NSAIDs, PPIs, and unnecessary antibiotics until clarified)
Assess volume status; provide isotonic fluids if hypovolemic and avoid nephrotoxins
Renal diet as indicated (limit potassium/phosphate if elevated; moderate sodium)
Monitor intake/output and daily weights; avoid contrast unless essential
Nephrology consult if creatinine rising, severe electrolyte/acid-base disturbance, oliguria, or uncertainty in diagnosis; consider kidney biopsy if diagnosis unclear or no improvement
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat basic metabolic panel (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 24–48 hours, then at least weekly until clear improvement; earlier (same day) if hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or rapid creatinine rise.

must_include: Kidney inflammation causing reduced filtering (AKI) and abnormal labs
Likely trigger is a medication exposure; stopping the trigger is key
Warning signs requiring urgent care: reduced urine output, swelling/shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, severe weakness/palpitations (possible hyperkalemia)
Immediate discontinuation/avoidance of suspected offending drugs and other nephrotoxins
Plan for close lab monitoring of creatinine, potassium, and bicarbonate
Steroids may be used if kidney function does not improve promptly or if diagnosis is confirmed/suspected strongly; discuss risks (infection, hyperglycemia, mood changes) and tapering
Review and document allergies (penicillin hives; TMP-SMX nausea/rash) and ensure alternatives are used
Discuss possibility of needing hospitalization/dialysis if severe hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload, or worsening kidney function occurs
Shared decision-making regarding biopsy/nephrology involvement if uncertainty persists
Avoid NSAIDs and non-prescribed supplements that may harm kidneys
Hydration guidance tailored to volume status (avoid dehydration; avoid excess fluids if overloaded)
Diet guidance if potassium is high (limit high-potassium foods) and sodium moderation to reduce fluid retention

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/AKI: timeline, medication exposures (esp. antibiotics/NSAIDs/PPIs), systemic symptoms (fever, rash, arthralgias), urine changes, volume status, and red flags.
Required safety check before any prescribing; confirm and document allergies and reaction types to guide safe alternatives.

Order initial AKI/nephritis workup and severity assessment: BMP (Cr/BUN, K, HCO3), CBC with eosinophils, UA with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio, urine sodium/FeNa (as appropriate), and consider complements/ANA/ANCA/anti-GBM if glomerulonephritis suspected.

Order contraindication-relevant tests before ordering potentially risky studies/therapies: pregnancy test if applicable; baseline vitals/weight; consider renal ultrasound if obstruction possible (ordering requires contraindication check per task).
Review labs to confirm AKI, identify nephritis features (pyuria/hematuria/proteinuria/casts), and detect urgent complications (hyperkalemia, acidosis).
Synthesize findings to diagnose likely nephritis (e.g., drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis vs glomerulonephritis) and stratify severity/need for admission or nephrology.
Non-critical but expected before prescribing to avoid worsening AKI/hyperkalemia (e.g., ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, TMP-SMX) and to ensure safe steroid/adjunct use.
Reconfirm allergies immediately before prescribing if any uncertainty remains; ensure planned meds avoid penicillins if hives and avoid TMP-SMX if prior nausea/rash unless benefits outweigh risks.
Critical check before prescribing: ensure proposed medications are appropriate in AKI/nephritis (renal dosing, avoid nephrotoxins; assess infection risk/contraindications to steroids).
Implement treatment: stop offending agent/nephrotoxins; prescribe supportive care and consider steroids when indicated; ensure renal-dose adjustments and monitoring plan.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, warning signs, allergy documentation, and lifestyle guidance (NSAID avoidance, hydration, potassium/sodium guidance).
Arrange close follow-up for repeat creatinine, potassium, bicarbonate and symptom reassessment; escalate to nephrology/biopsy discussion if no improvement or uncertainty.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

allergies and reactions (penicillin hives; TMP-SMX nausea/rash)
steps: ASK: focused AKI/nephritis history incl. meds, symptoms, red flags
CHECK_ALLERGY: document allergies and reaction types
ORDER_LAB: BMP, CBC with eosinophils, UA w/ microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio (± urine electrolytes)
GET_RESULTS: identify AKI and nephritis features; confirm no emergent hyperkalemia/acidosis/fluid overload
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds for nephrotoxins/hyperkalemia risk
contraindication_check (before prescribing): renal dosing; steroid contraindications if considering
PRESCRIBE: stop offending drug; avoid NSAIDs; supportive plan; consider steroids if no improvement/strong suspicion after discussion
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment, warning signs, lifestyle guidance

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking parent of a 6-month-old boy. Start with: “My baby keeps getting sick and just won’t shake this cough and diarrhea.” Early, volunteer: cough for weeks that keeps coming back, loose stools most days for a month, poor weight gain/smaller than other babies, tired/fussy, fevers on and off. If asked, add: recurrent thrush despite medicine, two ear infections, fast breathing/short of breath when cough flares, only brief improvement with antibiotics, stubborn diaper rash, slow-healing skin infection, frequent illnesses from daycare. Do not mention unless specifically prompted: few swollen glands, tiny/absent-looking tonsils, watery diarrhea worse after new foods/formula, fewer wet diapers during bad diarrhea, worse after mild colds others recover from. Share misconceptions: “Maybe allergies—worse at night,” “maybe lactose intolerance,” “could it be asthma—sometimes wheezes.” If vaccines come up, say you got very scared because he became much sicker after the last vaccine; you’re nervous but willing to discuss. Bring up financial worries if treatment plans are discussed. Respond to empathy by thanking them and becoming more open.

medications: Immune globulin (IVIG) 400 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 5 mg/kg/day TMP equivalent PO daily
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg PO once weekly
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO three times daily
comorbidities: Chronic diarrhea with suspected Giardia exposure
confounders: Recent systemic corticosteroid course for presumed asthma exacerbation (transient immunosuppression masking baseline)
Incomplete/unknown vaccination history due to frequent illness and missed visits
Recurrent daycare exposure and multiple viral infections complicating attribution of symptoms
Possible protein-calorie malnutrition contributing to low immunoglobulins and poor growth independent of SCID

diagnosis.primary: T-B+ severe combined immunodeficiency due to CD3delta/CD3epsilon/CD3zeta

correct_diagnosis.primary: T-B+ severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) due to CD3 delta/epsilon/zeta (CD3D/CD3E/CD247) deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: T-cell negative SCID (T−B+NK+ phenotype) due to TCR/CD3 complex defect
Combined immunodeficiency with profound T-cell lymphopenia (suspected SCID pending confirmatory immunophenotyping/genetics)
Severe T-cell lymphopenia concerning for SCID
required_evidence: Markedly low absolute lymphocyte count (age-adjusted lymphopenia) consistent with profound T-cell deficiency
Clinical history of severe/recurrent/opportunistic infections, persistent thrush/diarrhea, or failure to thrive (if provided in case stem)
No prior live vaccines given or concern for adverse events after live vaccines (if applicable)
Need for confirmatory immune workup: flow cytometry lymphocyte subsets (very low/absent T cells with preserved B cells), immunoglobulin levels, and genetic confirmation for CD3D/CD3E/CD247

expected_lab_results.WBC: May be normal or low; total WBC can be misleading because lymphocytes are profoundly reduced
expected_lab_results.Absolute lymphocyte count (ALC): Markedly decreased for age (severe lymphopenia; often <1000/µL in infants, can be far lower)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal initially; may be low if chronic illness or infection
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be low if severe sepsis or marrow suppression
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Often normal; may be elevated with bacterial infection or low with severe infection/marrow suppression
expected_lab_results.CRP: May be elevated if concurrent bacterial infection; can be low/variable in immunodeficiency
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: May be elevated with bacterial sepsis; may be low/variable otherwise
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be normal; may be low in dehydration/SIADH during infection

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (trimethoprim component) PO divided BID 3 days/week for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily for antifungal prophylaxis (alternative per local protocol)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO QID for HSV/VZV prophylaxis if seropositive exposure risk or per institutional protocol
Immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (or SCIG equivalent) to maintain protective IgG troughs

If febrile/ill-appearing: Cefepime 50 mg/kg IV q8h for empiric broad-spectrum coverage (avoid beta-lactams containing amoxicillin due to allergy history; cefepime generally acceptable unless history of severe immediate beta-lactam anaphylaxis)
If concern for MRSA or line infection: Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV q6h (dose/interval per age, renal function, and trough/AUC monitoring)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to immunology and transplant team for definitive therapy planning (hematopoietic stem cell transplantation; consider gene-based approaches if available/appropriate)
Strict infection prevention: protective isolation as indicated, meticulous hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts and crowded settings
Use CMV-negative, leukoreduced, irradiated blood products only (to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD and CMV transmission)

Avoid all live vaccines for patient; ensure household contacts are up-to-date with inactivated vaccines; discuss live vaccines in household contacts per immunology guidance (generally avoid oral polio; caution with rotavirus exposure)
Nutritional support and growth monitoring; evaluate and treat oral thrush/diarrhea promptly
Prompt evaluation of any fever (e.g., ≥38.0°C) as a medical emergency with low threshold for admission and IV antimicrobials
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day (urgent) immunology/transplant coordination; if stable on prophylaxis/IVIG, reassess within 1 week and then at least every 2-4 weeks until definitive therapy

must_include: SCID is a severe, life-threatening immune system problem with very low/absent functional T cells; B cells may be present but do not work well without T-cell help
High risk of serious infections even with mild symptoms; fever is an emergency
Definitive confirmation requires immune testing and genetic confirmation; this subtype involves the CD3/T-cell receptor complex (CD3 delta/epsilon/zeta)
Immediate infection prevention and prophylactic antimicrobials plus immunoglobulin replacement are supportive measures
Definitive cure is typically hematopoietic stem cell transplant (timing is urgent; best outcomes with early transplant)
Use only irradiated, CMV-negative, leukoreduced blood products if transfusion is needed
Avoid live vaccines for the patient until cleared by immunology
Review allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; egg ingestion causes hives—discuss implications for medications/vaccines and when to seek emergency care for anaphylaxis
Discuss warning signs requiring immediate care: fever, breathing difficulty, poor feeding, lethargy, new rash, decreased urine output
Set expectations for hospitalization/IV antibiotics if fever or ill appearance occurs
Infection control at home: hand hygiene, limit visitors, avoid daycare/crowds, mask use per local guidance, prompt evaluation of exposures
Household vaccination strategy: keep close contacts vaccinated with inactivated vaccines; discuss any live vaccines for contacts with immunology
Medication adherence for prophylaxis and IVIG schedule; do not stop without specialist input

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to assess current stability, infection risk, prior infections, vaccination status, exposures, family history/consanguinity, and confirm known allergies before any orders/prescriptions.

Order initial immune evaluation and infection workup appropriate for suspected SCID (e.g., CBC with differential/ALC, lymphocyte subsets including T/B/NK, immunoglobulins, T-cell function if available, cultures/viral testing if symptomatic), and request genetic confirmation pathway for CD3 complex defects.
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify details and severity of reported amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria and egg-related hives; document reaction type and any anaphylaxis features.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing prophylactic antimicrobials/adjuncts; screen for relevant interactions with any current medications/supplements.

Initiate supportive/prophylactic regimen per immunology guidance and clinical status (e.g., antimicrobial prophylaxis and IVIG planning) while avoiding contraindicated agents and ensuring allergy-informed selection.

Critical safety check before ordering any additional interventions: ensure avoidance of live vaccines, confirm blood product requirements (irradiated, CMV-negative, leukoreduced), and verify no contraindications to planned tests/therapies.
Review returned immune/infectious results to support diagnosis classification (T-B+ phenotype, CD3 complex involvement) and triage urgency.
State working diagnosis of T-B+ SCID due to CD3delta/epsilon/zeta pathway pending confirmatory functional/genetic testing; document severity and emergency precautions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (supportive + urgent HSCT referral), allergy implications, red-flag symptoms, infection-control lifestyle guidance, and household vaccination strategy.
Arrange urgent immunology/HSCT center coordination, IVIG scheduling, and clear return precautions; plan close follow-up for results and prophylaxis adherence.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and next steps.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been getting these bad headaches and my vision feels off.” Early in the visit, volunteer: pressure-like headaches behind your eyes that are increasing over months, intermittent blurred vision (worse reading/screens), and that you’ve bumped into things on the side. Also mention fatigue.

If asked about associated symptoms, share: brief double vision when looking to the side, nausea with worst headaches, irregular periods for months, and once or twice milky nipple discharge (not pregnant/breastfeeding), lower sex drive, and sometimes increased thirst/urination at night.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: needing brighter light/larger font, tighter rings/puffier face, constipation/dry skin, and occasional lightheadedness on standing.

Only disclose if the clinician asks gently about sexual symptoms: vaginal dryness, discomfort with sex, and difficulty reaching orgasm; you feel embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you thought migraines, needing new glasses, or stress/anxiety. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, answer more fully, and ask about non-surgical options and MRI/insurance costs.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for headache (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
Combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol 30 mcg/levonorgestrel 150 mcg) 1 tablet PO daily
confounders: SSRI-associated hyperprolactinemia (sertraline may elevate prolactin and contribute to galactorrhea/amenorrhea)
Combined oral contraceptive use can mask menstrual irregularity and complicate assessment of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
Chronic NSAID use for headaches may obscure progression of mass-effect symptoms
Mild hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine may suggest SIADH or secondary adrenal insufficiency; can complicate interpretation of pituitary axis testing

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary-region epidermoid or dermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar inclusion cyst)
acceptable_alternatives: Non-functioning pituitary lesion with mass effect (pending MRI characterization)
required_evidence: MRI sellar/suprasellar lesion with imaging characteristics consistent with epidermoid/dermoid (epidermoid: diffusion restriction; dermoid: T1 hyperintense fat signal ± fat droplets; both typically non-enhancing or minimal rim enhancement)
Symptoms/signs of mass effect and/or hypopituitarism (e.g., headache, visual field deficits, fatigue, menstrual/sexual dysfunction) OR incidental lesion requiring endocrine and visual assessment
Pituitary hormone evaluation showing no biochemical evidence of a functioning adenoma (no marked hormone hypersecretion pattern on available labs)
Neuro-ophthalmologic assessment (formal visual fields) if lesion abuts/compresses optic chiasm

expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal or mildly elevated due to stalk effect (typically modest elevation rather than very high levels seen in prolactinoma)
expected_lab_results.AM Cortisol (08:00): May be low or indeterminate if secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypopituitarism; can be normal if axis preserved
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal if secondary adrenal insufficiency; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypothyroidism; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low if central hypothyroidism; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Low or normal (may be low with growth hormone deficiency); not elevated
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypogonadism; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.FSH: Low or inappropriately normal if central hypogonadism; otherwise normal

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO qAM and 5 mg PO mid-afternoon (ONLY if secondary adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed; give before starting levothyroxine)
non_pharmacological: Urgent neurosurgery referral for evaluation of surgical resection/decompression if symptomatic, enlarging, or causing visual compromise; typical approach is endoscopic transsphenoidal resection when feasible
Endocrinology referral for full pituitary axis assessment and ongoing management
Formal visual field testing and ophthalmology/neuro-ophthalmology evaluation if lesion is near optic apparatus or if visual symptoms present
MRI brain/pituitary with and without contrast including diffusion-weighted imaging to confirm epidermoid/dermoid characteristics and define surgical anatomy
If central hypothyroidism confirmed: start levothyroxine after adrenal insufficiency is excluded or treated (dose individualized; monitor by Free T4 rather than TSH)
If hypogonadism present and not seeking fertility: consider sex hormone replacement after specialist evaluation; if fertility desired: gonadotropin-based therapy under endocrinology
If acute severe headache, meningismus, or sudden neurologic deterioration suggesting rupture/chemical meningitis: urgent ED evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for endocrine and neurosurgical review (sooner within 24-72 hours if visual deficits, suspected adrenal insufficiency, or acute neurologic symptoms); post-op endocrine follow-up within 1-2 weeks and repeat MRI per neurosurgery (often 3-6 months).

must_include: These cysts are typically benign, slow-growing inclusion cysts in/near the pituitary region (dermoid/epidermoid) and are different from most pituitary tumors that secrete hormones
Main risks are pressure effects (vision changes/headache) and possible reduced pituitary hormone production
Diagnosis relies on MRI characteristics (including diffusion imaging) and correlation with symptoms and hormone tests
Definitive management is usually surgical removal/decompression when symptomatic, affecting vision, or growing; surgery aims to relieve pressure and obtain diagnosis
Hormone deficiencies are treated with replacement; if adrenal insufficiency is possible, steroids must be started before thyroid hormone to avoid adrenal crisis
Need for visual field testing and ongoing MRI/endocrine follow-up even after treatment because recurrence can occur

Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: worsening vision, severe sudden headache, confusion, fainting, vomiting, fever/neck stiffness (possible rupture/chemical meningitis), or signs of adrenal crisis (severe weakness, low blood pressure)
Discuss expected course, surgical risks/benefits, and that some hormone issues may persist or newly occur after surgery requiring monitoring
If on glucocorticoids: stress-dose education (sick-day rules), do not stop abruptly, carry medical alert information
Medication adherence and timing for hormone replacement (if prescribed) and importance of follow-up labs/imaging
Avoid driving if experiencing significant visual impairment until evaluated

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline; screen for red flags (vision loss, severe sudden headache, fever/neck stiffness, syncope/vomiting), pituitary hormone deficiency/excess symptoms, and pregnancy status if relevant.
Focused neuro-ophthalmic history (visual acuity changes, bitemporal field loss, diplopia), headache features, and medication history including steroids/thyroid hormone use.

Order pituitary axis evaluation to assess hypopituitarism and guide safe hormone replacement (AM cortisol/ACTH, TSH/free T4, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH with estradiol/testosterone; electrolytes/Na+; pregnancy test when applicable).
If adrenal insufficiency suspected, prioritize urgent cortisol/ACTH and basic metabolic panel; consider glucose and CBC if systemic symptoms.
Interpret endocrine labs for hypopituitarism; identify adrenal insufficiency risk before any thyroid hormone is initiated.
Confirm imaging history and key MRI features if already done; if not, assess readiness/contraindications for MRI and contrast (implants, renal disease, claustrophobia).
If MRI with contrast is anticipated and renal function is unknown, order creatinine/eGFR per local protocol.

State most likely diagnosis: pituitary/suprasellar dermoid or epidermoid cyst based on clinical picture and MRI characteristics (including diffusion restriction for epidermoid) with differential noted (Rathke cleft cyst, craniopharyngioma, nonfunctioning adenoma).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (benign inclusion cysts, pressure effects, hormone deficits, MRI/DWI role); explain treatment (surgery when symptomatic/growing/vision threatened; hormone replacement; steroids before thyroid if AI risk; visual fields and follow-up); address concerns/red flags; lifestyle education (stress dosing, adherence, avoid driving with visual impairment).

Arrange urgent referrals as indicated: neuro-ophthalmology for formal visual fields, neurosurgery and endocrinology; plan interval MRI and endocrine labs; set urgency based on vision changes or red flags.
Required safety check before prescribing any medication (e.g., glucocorticoids, levothyroxine, desmopressin, analgesics).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review interactions (e.g., levothyroxine with iron/calcium, glucocorticoids with diabetes meds/NSAIDs, DDAVP with hyponatremia-risk meds).

Only if indicated: initiate stress-dose/physiologic glucocorticoids when adrenal insufficiency is suspected/confirmed; start thyroid hormone only after steroid coverage if AI possible; treat other deficiencies per results; provide clear instructions and emergency precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/OTC/supplements and alcohol/cannabis use for interaction risk with hypnotics (CNS depression, CYP interactions).

Start with: “I’ve had this cough that just won’t go away, and I get winded doing simple stuff.” Volunteer: dry cough ~6 weeks that keeps returning even after antibiotics, new shortness of breath climbing one flight of stairs, marked fatigue, and low-grade evening fevers. atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
omeprazole 20 mg PO daily

acceptable_alternatives: Secondary organizing pneumonia (if a trigger is later identified)
required_evidence: Subacute (days to weeks) persistent cough and dyspnea with systemic symptoms (e.g., malaise/low-grade fever) and failure to respond to empiric antibiotics
Imaging pattern consistent with organizing pneumonia (e.g., patchy bilateral consolidations/ground-glass opacities, often peripheral or peribronchovascular; migratory opacities may be present)
Infectious workup not supporting bacterial pneumonia (e.g., low/normal procalcitonin, no clear bacterial source)
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP/ESR) with no alternative explanation
Exclusion of secondary causes (medication-induced, connective tissue disease, aspiration, malignancy, radiation) to support “cryptogenic”

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often mild leukocytosis); not typically markedly elevated as in severe bacterial pneumonia
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal or mild anemia of inflammation
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (argues against typical bacterial pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal (may be mildly low in systemic illness, not a defining feature)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal (baseline; important before/with corticosteroids due to hypokalemia risk)

medications: Prednisone 0.75 mg/kg PO daily (typical 40-60 mg daily) for 2-4 weeks, then begin gradual taper over 3-6 months (longer taper if relapse)

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 800/160 mg PO three times weekly for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis if prednisone-equivalent >=20 mg/day expected for >=4 weeks (or if additional immunosuppression/risk factors)
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI protection if high-dose steroids and/or GI risk factors)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and exclude infection/secondary causes before committing to prolonged steroids (review exposures/medications, consider autoimmune screening, and ensure appropriate cultures/imaging review)
Pulmonary function testing and baseline oxygen saturation; provide supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic
Vaccination review and update (influenza, pneumococcal) prior to or early in immunosuppression when feasible
Bone protection counseling during steroid course (adequate calcium/vitamin D; consider osteoporosis risk assessment and bisphosphonate if indicated)
Arrange pulmonology follow-up; consider bronchoscopy with BAL and/or lung biopsy if diagnosis remains uncertain or poor response to steroids
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks to confirm symptomatic improvement and review labs/side effects; repeat chest imaging in 4-8 weeks and adjust taper based on clinical and radiographic response.

must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition (not cancer) where small airways/alveoli fill with organizing inflammatory tissue
Often mimics pneumonia but typically does not improve with antibiotics
Diagnosis requires excluding infection and other secondary causes; imaging pattern supports the diagnosis
Main treatment is corticosteroids; improvement is often seen within days to 1-2 weeks
Need for a slow taper over months to reduce relapse risk
Potential steroid side effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes/insomnia, weight gain, infection risk, hypertension, bone loss) and what to report urgently
Discuss uncertainty and contingency plan if symptoms worsen or no improvement (re-evaluate for infection, alternative diagnoses, consider bronchoscopy/biopsy)
Safety-net symptoms requiring urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, hypoxemia, high fever, chest pain, hemoptysis)
Clarify allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and implications for future antibiotic choices
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, prompt evaluation for fever)
Adherence to taper schedule; do not stop steroids abruptly
Bone health measures during steroid therapy (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise as tolerated) and follow-up testing as advised

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for COP evaluation: subacute cough/dyspnea, fever, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis; prior antibiotic nonresponse; exposure history; autoimmune symptoms; medication/radiation history; smoking/vaping; aspiration risk; infectious risk (TB/fungal); baseline function and oxygen needs; pregnancy status if relevant.

Order initial evaluation to exclude infection/secondary causes and assess severity: CBC with differential, CMP, CRP/ESR, procalcitonin (if available), respiratory viral PCR, sputum culture (if productive), blood cultures if febrile, HIV if risk, autoimmune screen if suggestive (ANA/RF/anti-CCP, myositis panel as indicated), and pulse oximetry/ABG if hypoxemic.
Order imaging and functional assessment supportive of COP and to gauge severity: chest X-ray and/or CT chest (if not already obtained), consider PFTs when stable.

Review results to ensure no clear bacterial/viral/fungal infection and that imaging pattern supports organizing pneumonia (e.g., patchy bilateral consolidations/ground-glass, peripheral or peribronchovascular distribution) and to identify red flags requiring escalation.

Make a working diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia only after reasonable exclusion of infection and secondary organizing pneumonia causes; state diagnostic uncertainty and contingency for bronchoscopy/biopsy if atypical course.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergy history (e.g., penicillin urticarial rash), reaction type/severity/timing, and implications for future antibiotic selection if needed.
Noncritical but expected before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned corticosteroids (e.g., warfarin, diabetes meds, NSAIDs, strong CYP3A4 inhibitors) and any prophylactic meds if used.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm no contraindications to systemic steroids (uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, active GI bleed/peptic ulcer, severe uncontrolled hypertension, psychosis, untreated TB, etc.), then prescribe oral prednisone (typical starting ~0.75–1 mg/kg/day or guideline-consistent equivalent) with a slow taper plan over months; consider adjuncts (PPI if GI risk, calcium/vit D; osteoporosis prophylaxis based on risk).

Meet communication milestones: explain COP diagnosis (inflammatory, not cancer; mimics pneumonia; excludes infection/secondary causes; imaging supports), explain steroid treatment and expected timeline, taper importance, side effects and urgent symptoms, infection prevention, and bone health measures.

Arrange close follow-up to assess response and safety: reassess in 1–2 weeks (or sooner if severe), repeat symptoms/vitals/SpO2, consider repeat imaging in 4–8 weeks, monitor glucose/BP, and adjust taper; establish contingency plan if no improvement.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm no contraindications to systemic steroids (uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, active GI bleed/peptic ulcer, severe uncontrolled hypertension, psychosis, untreated TB, etc.), then prescribe oral prednisone (typical starting ~0.75–1 mg/kg/day or guideline-consistent equivalent) with a slow taper plan over months; consider adjuncts (PPI if GI risk, calcium/vit D; osteoporosis prophylaxis based on risk).

Meet communication milestones: explain COP diagnosis (inflammatory, not cancer; mimics pneumonia; excludes infection/secondary causes; imaging supports), explain steroid treatment and expected timeline, taper importance, side effects and urgent symptoms, infection prevention, and bone health measures.

Arrange close follow-up to assess response and safety: reassess in 1–2 weeks (or sooner if severe), repeat symptoms/vitals/SpO2, consider repeat imaging in 4–8 weeks, monitor glucose/BP, and adjust taper; establish contingency plan if no improvement.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking HR manager. Start by saying: “I’ve had burning, stabbing, electric pain deep in my pelvis/groin that gets much worse when I sit.” Volunteer that sitting >10–15 minutes flares it, standing/lying helps, it’s mostly right-sided near the sit bone/inner buttock, and it’s been months and exhausting.

If asked, add: numb/tingly around genitals/anus; shooting pain toward vagina and rectum; donut cushion helps a bit; bowel movements/straining trigger flares; sex can be painful or cause hours of pain after; urinary frequency on bad days but UTI tests/cultures negative; mornings better, worsens through the day with sitting.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: avoiding car rides/restaurants/work; pelvic muscle tensing; altered sitting/walking with hip/back tightness; “golf ball” rectal pressure.

Only with direct, sensitive questions disclose: sharp pain with arousal/orgasm; trouble starting/finishing urination; feeling anxious/down and relationship strain.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s a stubborn UTI, hemorrhoids, or a slipped disc; antifungal cream didn’t help.

Show worry about costs/coverage and fear job loss if you report it. If clinician is empathetic, soften, share more, and ask for reassurance and a plan.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime
Estradiol 0.01% vaginal cream 1 g intravaginally twice weekly
confounders: History of endometriosis with chronic pelvic pain overlap
Pelvic floor muscle spasm/myofascial pain mimicking neuropathic pain
Functional constipation causing pelvic pressure and pain exacerbation
Perimenopausal genitourinary syndrome (vulvovaginal dryness/dyspareunia)
Anxiety/depression affecting pain perception and reporting
Prolonged sitting for work (risk factor and symptom trigger)
Prior pelvic surgery (laparoscopy) raising concern for adhesions/other pelvic pathology

acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with suspected pudendal neuropathy
required_evidence: Neuropathic pain (burning/shooting/tingling) in pudendal nerve distribution (perineum, vulva/scrotum, anus, distal urethra)
Pain worsened by sitting and improved by standing/lying
No objective sensory loss on exam (or minimal) and no other neurologic red flags
Tenderness over pudendal nerve course (e.g., ischial spine/Alcock canal) and/or pain reproduced with palpation
Symptoms present for weeks to months and not explained by infection, mass, or primary spinal pathology
Supportive response to diagnostic pudendal nerve block (if performed) OR improvement with pelvic floor–directed therapy/neuropathic pain regimen

patient.instructions:

Do not mention snoring/apnea, dry mouth/headache, nocturia, leg restlessness, sweating/palpitations, or stress unless asked about breathing, morning symptoms, bathroom trips, leg sensations, panic, or stress.

confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes) contributing to sleep fragmentation

non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) (stimulus control, sleep restriction/extension as appropriate, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary/ISI, adherence to CBT-I strategies, medication efficacy/tolerability; earlier (within 1 week) if significant sedation, falls, or safety issues

Before prescribing any hypnotic, verify allergies and reactions; explicitly confirm reported penicillin/shellfish allergy details and document that typical insomnia meds are not cross-reactive but still require verification.

general_concerns: Because my dad had 'cluster migraines,' I assume this is basically the same as migraine and that migraine medicines should always work.
I think the headaches mean something is wrong with my brain, like an aneurysm or tumor, because the pain is so sudden and severe.
I believe oxygen is dangerous or addictive and could 'weaken my lungs,' so I’m hesitant to use it even if it helps.
I’m convinced stress is the main cause and that if I can just sleep more or relax enough, the attacks will stop completely.
I’ve read that frequent use of nasal sprays or painkillers can 'cause' cluster headaches, so I avoid taking anything early and wait until the pain is unbearable.
I think if I can identify a single food trigger (like chocolate or cheese) and eliminate it, the clusters will go away permanently.
economic_concerns: I’m worried home oxygen and specialist visits will be expensive and not covered, so I’d rather try cheaper over-the-counter options first.
I’m concerned that taking time off for repeated appointments or ER visits will threaten my job because of my shift schedule.
I assume newer treatments or injections will be unaffordable and that insurance will require me to 'fail' multiple medications before approving them.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking IT support specialist on shift work. Start with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches around my left eye that come in attacks.” Volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the left eye in sudden waves peaking within minutes; left eye red/tearing; left nostril stuffy/runny; you can’t sit still—pace/restless.

If asked, add: lasts ~45 minutes to 2 hours; several attacks/day for a few weeks then months off; often wakes you at the same time at night; little light/sound sensitivity; alcohol triggers during “bad stretches”; eyelid droop and smaller pupil on that side during attacks; dad had “suicide headaches.”

Withhold unless specifically asked about mood/substances: pre-cluster insomnia/on-edge, increased smoking, springtime pattern, post-attack exhaustion. If asked directly, admit more drinking (worsens), tried a friend’s strong pain pills, and occasional scary “can’t do this” thoughts—no plan.

Mention misconceptions: worry it’s sinus/tooth; electric shock jabs and touch/teeth brushing can trigger. Express cost/job fears and preference for OTC first. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Nicotine polacrilex gum 2 mg buccal PRN cravings (max 24 pieces/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6h PRN pain (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
allergies: Sumatriptan (anaphylaxis: throat tightness and wheeze within 15 minutes of first dose, required ED treatment)
confounders: Migraine without aura history in adolescence (overlapping unilateral headache features)
Chronic rhinosinusitis/allergic rhinitis symptoms (nasal congestion and tearing can mimic autonomic features)
Tobacco and episodic alcohol use (alcohol triggers attacks during cluster periods)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (snoring, overweight; nocturnal headaches can complicate pattern recognition)

acceptable_alternatives: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia - cluster headache phenotype
required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe or very severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes (untreated)
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day during a cluster period

At least one ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptom (conjunctival injection/lacrimation, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, forehead/facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation during attacks
Stereotyped attacks with circadian/circannual pattern and/or family history consistent with familial cluster headache

No better alternative diagnosis (including trigeminal neuralgia: brief electric-shock pains lasting seconds, triggered by touch/chewing, with refractory period and without prominent autonomic features/restlessness)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected unless intercurrent infection/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (needed as baseline if considering verapamil monitoring or other therapies)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset of attack for 15-20 minutes PRN
Zolmitriptan 5 mg intranasal at onset of attack; may repeat once after 2 hours (max 10 mg/24 h) (ONLY if no triptan class allergy—contraindicated here due to sumatriptan anaphylaxis)
Dihydroergotamine 1 mg IM/IV at onset of attack (avoid if significant cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, pregnancy; ensure no recent triptan/ergot use)
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge therapy while preventive starts)
Verapamil 80 mg PO three times daily, titrate every 7-14 days as tolerated toward 240-480 mg/day (or higher per specialist) with ECG monitoring
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; screen for and treat sleep apnea if suspected
Headache diary to track attack timing, triggers, and response to therapy
Neurology/headache specialist referral for preventive titration and consideration of alternatives (e.g., galcanezumab, lithium, greater occipital nerve block) if inadequate control
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks (earlier within 3-7 days if frequent attacks, medication side effects, or inadequate response); urgent evaluation for red flags or atypical features

must_include: Cluster headache is a trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia causing severe one-sided attacks with autonomic symptoms and restlessness
How it differs from trigeminal neuralgia (cluster lasts 15–180 minutes with autonomic features; trigeminal neuralgia is seconds-long electric shocks with triggers)
Familial tendency can occur; not due to dangerous brain pathology when typical features present, but atypical/red flag features warrant evaluation
Two-part plan: acute abortive treatment (high-flow oxygen; other options) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and a short steroid bridge
Explicitly state that sumatriptan is contraindicated due to prior anaphylaxis and that other triptans may also be unsafe; discuss alternative abortives
Expected onset and how to use oxygen early in an attack; when to seek urgent care (new neurologic deficits, worst headache, fever/neck stiffness, chest pain, allergic symptoms)
Review prior anaphylaxis details and provide an avoidance plan for sumatriptan/triptan class pending allergy specialist input
Discuss verapamil risks (bradycardia, hypotension, constipation) and need for ECG monitoring during titration
Discuss steroid short-term risks (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, GI upset) and taper plan
Avoid alcohol during cluster bouts; identify and avoid individual triggers
Sleep hygiene and regular schedule; consider evaluation for sleep apnea if symptoms
Use a headache diary and adhere to follow-up for preventive titration and monitoring

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule in cluster headache: unilateral severe orbital/temporal pain, duration 15–180 min, frequency (up to 8/day), circadian/circannual pattern, ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis), and marked restlessness; assess triggers (alcohol), family history, and prior similar bouts.

Screen for secondary headache/red flags and comorbidities relevant to treatment: sudden thunderclap, new neuro deficits, fever/neck stiffness, immunosuppression, pregnancy, age >50 new onset, cancer history; assess cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, arrhythmia, asthma/COPD (oxygen), and current meds/substances.

Critical before prescribing: confirm prior anaphylaxis to sumatriptan, reaction details, timing, and other drug allergies; establish avoidance plan for sumatriptan and caution with triptan class pending specialist input.
Make diagnosis of (familial) cluster headache based on typical features; differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia and migraine; determine episodic vs chronic pattern if possible.

Critical before prescribing: review current medications for interactions with proposed preventives/bridges (e.g., verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin, CYP3A4 inhibitors; steroids with diabetes meds/NSAIDs; avoid ergot with interacting vasoconstrictors).

If considering any abortive other than oxygen (e.g., intranasal lidocaine, DHE, non-triptan options), ensure no interacting serotonergic/vasoconstrictive agents and verify compatibility with comorbidities.

Non-critical before ordering: assess contraindications to ordering high-flow oxygen (e.g., severe CO2 retention risk) and to obtaining baseline ECG prior to verapamil titration; confirm no barriers to safe home oxygen use.

Order baseline ECG (and repeat with dose increases) for verapamil initiation/titration; consider pregnancy test if applicable before steroids/other meds; consider glucose/A1c if diabetes risk prior to steroid bridge (site-dependent).
Review ECG for bradycardia/AV block/QT issues before starting or up-titrating verapamil; confirm no contraindicating findings.

Critical before prescribing: confirm no contraindications to verapamil (significant bradycardia, hypotension, high-grade AV block without pacer, severe heart failure with reduced EF) and to steroid bridge (uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, severe psychiatric history, active GI ulcer/bleed risk).

Initiate evidence-based treatment plan: acute abortive high-flow oxygen (non-rebreather 12–15 L/min for ~15–20 min at onset; may adjust per protocol) and preventive verapamil with ECG monitoring and titration; prescribe short steroid bridge with taper; explicitly avoid sumatriptan and generally avoid triptans due to anaphylaxis history; consider alternative abortives (e.g., intranasal lidocaine; DHE only if appropriate and no vascular contraindications).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and differences from trigeminal neuralgia; explain two-part treatment plan, oxygen use and expected onset, and urgent return precautions; address concerns about anaphylaxis avoidance, verapamil risks/ECG monitoring, and steroid risks/taper; provide lifestyle counseling (avoid alcohol during bouts, sleep regularity, headache diary, trigger identification).

Arrange close follow-up for verapamil titration and ECG checks (often within 1–2 weeks and with each dose increase), review diary/response, and consider neurology/headache specialist and allergy referral regarding triptan class reaction.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits core cluster headache features (unilateral severe pain, 15–180 min duration, autonomic symptoms, restlessness, attack frequency/pattern) and distinguishes from trigeminal neuralgia (seconds-long electric shocks with triggers).

Agent screens for red flags/secondary causes and states that typical cluster features are not usually due to dangerous brain pathology, while acknowledging atypical/red-flag features warrant evaluation.

Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check and correctly identifies prior sumatriptan anaphylaxis as a contraindication to sumatriptan; agent cautions that other triptans may also be unsafe pending allergy specialist input.

Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication check and avoids contraindicated therapies (e.g., triptans/ergots in significant vascular disease; verapamil in high-grade AV block/bradycardia/hypotension; steroids when unsafe).
Before prescribing, agent performs a drug interaction check for verapamil and other proposed medications.

Agent proposes appropriate acute therapy emphasizing high-flow oxygen with clear instructions (start early at onset, non-rebreather, adequate flow, typical duration) and offers non-triptan alternatives given anaphylaxis history.

Agent proposes appropriate preventive therapy with verapamil and includes ECG monitoring during titration; mentions key adverse effects (bradycardia, hypotension, constipation) and what to do if they occur.

minimal_paths.description: Typical familial cluster headache with prior sumatriptan anaphylaxis: diagnose clinically, abort with oxygen, start verapamil with ECG monitoring, add short steroid bridge, counsel and arrange close follow-up.
must_collect: red flag symptoms (thunderclap, neuro deficit, fever/neck stiffness)
cardiac history and baseline vitals symptoms (bradycardia/syncope)
steps: ASK: characterize headaches; confirm cluster phenotype and familial pattern; screen red flags and comorbidities
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm sumatriptan anaphylaxis; counsel avoidance
DIAGNOSE: cluster headache (likely episodic if bouts with remissions)
CHECK_INTERACTION: evaluate current meds for verapamil/steroid interactions
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: assess suitability for oxygen/verapamil/steroids; plan baseline ECG
ORDER_LAB: baseline ECG (and plan repeat ECGs with titration)
GET_RESULTS: confirm ECG acceptable for verapamil
PRESCRIBE: high-flow oxygen abortive + verapamil preventive + short steroid bridge/taper; avoid triptans; consider alternative abortive options
EDUCATE: cover diagnosis, treatment, safety-net, lifestyle, monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: early review for titration/ECG and response; consider neurology/allergy referral

minimal_paths.description: Headache features suggest cluster but with atypical/red-flag elements; agent still avoids triptans due to anaphylaxis and initiates safe acute therapy while arranging urgent evaluation/imaging as appropriate (site-dependent).
must_collect: cluster-like features (unilateral severe pain, autonomic symptoms, restlessness)
atypical features (new onset >50, persistent neuro deficits, thunderclap, systemic symptoms)
steps: ASK: characterize headaches; identify red flags/atypical features
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm sumatriptan anaphylaxis; avoid triptans
DIAGNOSE: probable cluster headache but note need to rule out secondary causes
CHECK_INTERACTION + CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: ensure safe interim therapy
PRESCRIBE: high-flow oxygen as abortive if safe; consider non-triptan alternatives as appropriate
EDUCATE: explain why further evaluation is needed; provide urgent precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedite in-person/ED or urgent neurology evaluation per red-flag severity

acceptable_variants.description: Agent uses oxygen as primary abortive and adds intranasal lidocaine as an alternative abortive (instead of DHE), while still starting verapamil and a steroid bridge with ECG monitoring and full counseling.
steps: PRESCRIBE (oxygen + verapamil + steroid bridge; intranasal lidocaine PRN; no triptans)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders ECG before prescribing verapamil (hard stop) and provides oxygen prescription immediately after allergy/contraindication checks; verapamil is started after ECG results reviewed.
Agent includes neurology/headache specialist referral and allergy referral as part of follow-up planning; otherwise standard pathway with oxygen, verapamil + ECG monitoring, and steroid bridge.

volunteer: Shortness of breath that comes on during these episodes
Feeling shaky and sweaty like my blood sugar is dropping
Sudden tiredness and weakness that can get bad fast
Nausea and sometimes throwing up when it happens

if_asked: I get confused and have trouble thinking straight during an episode
My heart feels like it’s racing when it happens
I get really irritable or anxious when I haven’t eaten
I sometimes get stomach pain and don’t want food
I’ve had episodes since I was little, especially with stomach bugs
After I eat or drink something sugary, I usually feel better within a bit
hidden: I breathe faster and deeper during the episodes, like I can’t catch my breath even though my chest isn’t tight
My breath sometimes smells kind of fruity or weird when I’m really unwell
I notice these episodes are worse after long exercise if I didn’t eat enough beforehand
I can go from ‘fine’ to ‘really sick’ in a couple of hours if I’m not able to keep food down
I’ve learned I do better with frequent meals and I crash if I try fasting or skipping breakfast
resistant: I sometimes drink regular soda or juice in secret to stop the shaky feeling because I’m embarrassed about needing it
I’ve been told in the past it might be ‘in my head’ or related to anxiety, so I don’t always tell people how bad it gets
I’ve avoided telling employers/teachers about it because I’m afraid it’ll affect how they see me
misleading: I’ve had tingling in my hands and around my mouth during episodes and worried it was a nerve problem
I’ve had bad headaches with feeling ‘foggy’ and worried it could be something like an infection in the brain
I bruise easily and I’m pretty flexible, so I wondered if I have a connective tissue problem
I’ve had occasional nosebleeds and thought it might be a blood disorder
noise: Seasonal allergies with a runny nose
Occasional mild back pain after sitting too long
A couple of nights a week I don’t sleep well
Sometimes my ears ring briefly after loud music

general_concerns: Thinks the shortness of breath means her lungs are failing, not realizing it can happen when her blood sugar is low or when she is dehydrated and breathing fast.
Believes she must avoid all fruit and anything sweet forever, and that even tiny accidental amounts will immediately be dangerous.
Thinks antibiotics like fusidic acid are 'strong' and can fix the metabolic problem, so she asks if taking it during a crisis will help her breathing.
Assumes skipping meals is healthy when she feels nauseated, not understanding that fasting can trigger her episodes.
Believes drinking juice is always the best treatment for feeling unwell, not realizing some juices contain fructose that can worsen her condition.
economic_concerns: Family worries that special diets and frequent snacks will be too expensive and asks if cheaper alternatives (like skipping snacks) are safe.
Caregiver is concerned about the cost of repeated emergency visits and asks if they can manage severe episodes at home to avoid hospital bills.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 6-year-old girl (answer simply, often “I don’t know”), acting anxious and a bit vague. Start by saying you suddenly get short of breath and shaky, especially when you haven’t eaten. Volunteer: shaky/sweaty like “blood sugar dropping,” sudden tired/weakness that can get bad fast, nausea/vomiting, and it happens after skipping meals or when sick and not eating.

Only if asked, add: confusion/trouble thinking, racing heart, irritability/anxiety when hungry, stomach pain/poor appetite, episodes since little (worse with stomach bugs), and you feel better after sugary drink/food.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: fast/deep breathing without chest tightness, fruity/odd breath, worse after long exercise without food, can go from fine to very sick in hours if can’t keep food down, frequent meals help and fasting/breakfast skipping makes you crash.

Resist admitting you secretly drink soda/juice from embarrassment; reveal only if reassured. Mention past adults said it’s “in your head/anxiety.” Bring up worries about tingling, headaches/foggy, easy bruising/flexibility, and nosebleeds if the clinician asks about other concerns. If empathy is shown, you calm slightly and share more. Ask about cheaper snack options and avoiding costly ER visits.

medications: Fusidic acid 2% topical ointment apply thin layer to affected area TID
Ondansetron 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea/vomiting
comorbidities: Recurrent fasting intolerance with prior episodes of hypoglycemia
confounders: Recent viral gastroenteritis with poor oral intake (prolonged fasting trigger)
Tachypnea from metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul-type breathing) mimicking primary pulmonary disease
Mild leukocytosis and elevated CRP suggesting infection/sepsis as an alternative cause of lactic acidosis
Non-ketotic hypoglycemia can be mistaken for insulin exposure or fatty acid oxidation disorder

correct_diagnosis.primary: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency (FBPase deficiency) presenting with hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis
acceptable_alternatives: Inborn error of metabolism causing fasting intolerance with lactic acidosis (suspected FBPase deficiency)
Glycogen storage disease type I (von Gierke) (as a metabolic alternative with similar labs)
required_evidence: Point-of-care glucose low (hypoglycemia), especially after fasting/illness
Venous blood gas shows metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3-)
Elevated lactate
Elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis
Dyspnea explained by compensatory hyperventilation from metabolic acidosis (low venous pCO2)

expected_lab_results.Point-of-care glucose: Low (often <70 mg/dL; may be markedly low in crisis)
expected_lab_results.Venous blood gas pH: Low (metabolic acidemia, e.g., <7.35; can be <7.25 if severe)
expected_lab_results.Venous HCO3-: Low (e.g., <18 mmol/L; can be <12 if severe)
expected_lab_results.pCO2 (venous): Low relative to baseline (respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Lactate: Elevated (often >2 mmol/L; may be >5 mmol/L in crisis)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (may reflect dehydration/illness)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to mildly elevated (shift with acidosis); monitor for changes during treatment

medications: Dextrose 10% IV infusion start 100-150 mL/hr (or weight-based to deliver ~6-8 mg/kg/min glucose) titrate to maintain glucose >80-100 mg/dL
Dextrose 50% IV 25 g bolus once if severe symptomatic hypoglycemia, then continue dextrose infusion
Normal saline 0.9% IV bolus 500-1000 mL once if clinically dehydrated, then reassess
Sodium bicarbonate IV 1-2 mEq/kg once only if severe acidemia (pH <= 7.1) or hemodynamic instability, with repeat VBG monitoring
non_pharmacological: Immediate avoidance of fasting; provide frequent carbohydrate intake once able to take PO
Avoid fructose, sucrose, and sorbitol-containing foods/medications; use glucose-based carbohydrate sources
Treat precipitating illness (e.g., infection) and ensure adequate hydration
Consult metabolic/genetics service urgently; initiate individualized emergency regimen and sick-day plan
Continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring if significant acidosis or altered work of breathing; consider ICU if pH very low, rising lactate, or unstable vitals
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck point-of-care glucose every 15-30 minutes until stable, then hourly; repeat VBG/lactate and electrolytes within 1-2 hours after starting therapy; metabolic specialist follow-up within 24-72 hours and establish written emergency plan before discharge

must_include: Dyspnea can be from the body breathing faster to compensate for metabolic acidosis
Condition involves impaired gluconeogenesis leading to low blood sugar and lactic acidosis during fasting/illness
Why differentials like Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, tick-borne encephalitis, and vascular Ehlers-Danlos are less consistent with the available labs/single symptom in this presentation
Immediate goal is to correct hypoglycemia with IV dextrose and stop ketotic/lactic crisis
Hydration and monitoring of acid-base status/electrolytes; bicarbonate only if acidosis is severe
Plan for repeat labs and criteria for escalation (worsening pH, rising lactate, persistent symptoms)
Review amoxicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and avoidance of penicillins unless evaluated
Discuss current medication (fusidic acid) and confirm no new medications contain fructose/sucrose/sorbitol excipients
Explain warning signs requiring urgent care: recurrent dyspnea, vomiting, inability to tolerate carbs, confusion, or low glucose symptoms
Strict avoidance of fasting; frequent meals/snacks and sick-day carbohydrate strategy
Avoid fructose/sucrose/sorbitol in foods and liquid medications
Need for an emergency letter/plan and medical alert identification

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency decompensation: fasting/illness trigger, carbohydrate intake, vomiting, dyspnea pattern, mental status, prior similar episodes, home glucose readings, dietary restrictions, and current meds/excipients.

Order urgent labs to assess hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis severity and guide escalation: point-of-care glucose, BMP/electrolytes, venous/arterial blood gas, lactate, beta-hydroxybutyrate/ketones, LFTs, ammonia, CBC, urinalysis/urine ketones; consider infection workup if febrile.

Before ordering any potentially harmful interventions, verify contraindications and avoid fructose/sucrose/sorbitol-containing IV/oral solutions or medications; ensure planned fluids/dextrose formulations are appropriate for the disorder.
Interpret glucose, pH/bicarbonate/anion gap, lactate, ketones, electrolytes to confirm decompensation and determine urgency (e.g., severe acidemia, rising lactate, refractory hypoglycemia).
Make/confirm diagnosis of acute metabolic decompensation in fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency and document why alternative differentials are less consistent with the available data.
Before prescribing, confirm and document allergy history (amoxicillin urticarial rash) and implications for antibiotic selection if infection is suspected.
Before prescribing, check interactions with current medication (fusidic acid) and planned agents; also screen for excipients (fructose/sucrose/sorbitol) in liquid meds.

Initiate treatment to reverse crisis: IV dextrose to correct hypoglycemia and suppress ketosis/lactic acidosis; isotonic fluids for hydration; electrolyte correction; consider bicarbonate only for severe acidemia per protocol.
Communicate diagnosis, mechanism, treatment plan, monitoring, escalation criteria, medication/excipient safety, sick-day rules, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange metabolic/genetics follow-up and provide/renew emergency letter/plan and medical alert guidance; plan repeat labs and follow-up timeframe based on severity.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment plan, and understanding of warning signs and follow-up.

Initiate treatment to reverse crisis: IV dextrose to correct hypoglycemia and suppress ketosis/lactic acidosis; isotonic fluids for hydration; electrolyte correction; consider bicarbonate only for severe acidemia per protocol.
Communicate diagnosis, mechanism, treatment plan, monitoring, escalation criteria, medication/excipient safety, sick-day rules, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange metabolic/genetics follow-up and provide/renew emergency letter/plan and medical alert guidance; plan repeat labs and follow-up timeframe based on severity.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment plan, and understanding of warning signs and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly links dyspnea to compensatory tachypnea from metabolic acidosis and does not default to primary pulmonary causes without evidence.

Agent explains that fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency causes impaired gluconeogenesis leading to hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis during fasting/illness, and ties this to the patient’s presentation.

Agent clearly states why Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, tick-borne encephalitis, and vascular Ehlers-Danlos are less consistent with the available labs/single symptom in this presentation (e.g., lack of characteristic systemic/neurologic/bleeding or connective-tissue findings and mismatch with metabolic pattern).
Agent prioritizes immediate correction of hypoglycemia with IV dextrose and states the goal to stop ketotic/lactic crisis.
Agent includes hydration plus monitoring of acid-base status and electrolytes; mentions bicarbonate only if acidosis is severe, not as routine therapy.
Agent defines a plan for repeat labs and explicit escalation criteria (e.g., worsening pH, rising lactate, persistent symptoms, inability to maintain euglycemia, altered mental status).

Agent performs required safety checks in the correct timing: contraindication check before ordering; allergy check before prescribing; interaction check before prescribing (non-critical but expected).
Agent reviews amoxicillin allergy as urticarial rash and advises avoidance of penicillins unless formally evaluated; avoids prescribing penicillins without justification.
Agent addresses current fusidic acid use and confirms no new medications (especially liquids) contain fructose/sucrose/sorbitol excipients.
Agent provides clear urgent care warning signs: recurrent dyspnea, vomiting, inability to tolerate carbohydrates, confusion/altered mental status, or symptoms/signs of low glucose.

Agent delivers lifestyle education: strict avoidance of fasting, frequent meals/snacks, sick-day carbohydrate strategy, avoidance of fructose/sucrose/sorbitol in foods and liquid medications, and need for emergency letter/plan and medical alert identification.
Agent avoids unsafe recommendations such as fasting, ketogenic/low-carb diets, or fructose-containing rehydration solutions; avoids delaying dextrose in symptomatic hypoglycemia.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize likely metabolic decompensation in fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency, confirm with key labs, treat promptly with IV dextrose and supportive care, and provide education and escalation plan.
must_collect: recent fasting duration or poor intake
vomiting and ability to tolerate carbohydrates
current medications and formulations (liquid vs tablet) including fusidic acid
steps: ASK: focused history for fasting/illness-triggered hypoglycemia/acidosis and medication/excipient review
ORDER_LAB: glucose, BMP, VBG/ABG, lactate, ketones, LFTs, ammonia ± CBC/UA
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: avoid fructose/sucrose/sorbitol-containing solutions/meds before ordering/administering
GET_RESULTS: assess severity (pH/bicarb, lactate, glucose, electrolytes, ketones)
DIAGNOSE: acute decompensation in fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency; explain why listed differentials are less consistent
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm amoxicillin urticarial rash before any prescribing
CHECK_INTERACTION: review fusidic acid and planned meds; screen excipients
PRESCRIBE: IV dextrose + isotonic fluids; electrolyte correction; bicarbonate only if severe acidemia; set monitoring/repeat labs
EDUCATE: diagnosis, dyspnea-as-acidosis compensation, treatment goals, repeat labs and escalation criteria, warning signs, excipient avoidance, sick-day plan
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: metabolic specialist follow-up and emergency plan/medical alert

acceptable_variants.description: Treat first in parallel with workup when symptomatic hypoglycemia is suspected (do not delay dextrose), then complete labs and diagnosis explanation.
steps: ASK: brief safety-critical history (fasting/illness, vomiting, mental status, meds/excipients, allergies)
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION: ensure dextrose/fluid choice avoids fructose/sucrose/sorbitol-containing products
PRESCRIBE: immediate IV dextrose and hydration while arranging tests
ORDER_LAB: glucose, BMP, VBG/ABG, lactate, ketones, LFTs, ammonia ± CBC/UA
GET_RESULTS: reassess and adjust therapy; define escalation criteria
DIAGNOSE: confirm decompensation and address differentials
CHECK_INTERACTION: before additional meds; excipient screening
EDUCATE: full counseling including warning signs and lifestyle/sick-day plan

acceptable_variants.description: If presentation is mild and labs show no severe acidemia, manage with close monitoring and carbohydrate strategy while still emphasizing emergency thresholds and excipient avoidance.
steps: EDUCATE: oral carbohydrate/sick-day plan if safe; explicit return precautions and escalation thresholds
PRESCRIBE: only if needed (e.g., antiemetic without contraindicated excipients; dextrose if hypoglycemic)
